perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees not to use the Confidential Information received by such party as a Receiving Party other than for the Purpose of this Agreement. Each party hereto shall: (a) not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party hereto to any other party and will use best efforts to protect the confidentiality of such information, (b) not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose of this Agreement, and (c) not {J1872881.2} 2 reproduce, copy or photograph any document or other medium which contains Confidential Information, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that each party hereto may furnish the other’s Confidential Information to those employees or representatives of such party who need to have access to such Confidential Information to assist such party in achieving the Purpose of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto agrees not to use the Confidential Information received by such party as a Receiving Party other than for the Purpose of this Agreement. Each party hereto shall: (a) endeavor not to disclose the Confidential Information of the other party hereto to any other party and will use reasonable efforts to protect the confidentiality of such information, (b) not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose of this Agreement, and (c) not {J1872881.2} 2 reproduce, copy or photograph any document or other medium which contains Confidential Information, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that each party hereto may furnish the other’s Confidential Information to those employees or representatives of such party who may have access to such Confidential Information to assist such party in understanding the Purpose of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing 'not disclose' with 'endeavor not to disclose' and 'best efforts' with 'reasonable efforts' reduces the standard of care required. Further, modifying 'need to have access' to 'may have access' and changing 'achieving the Purpose' to 'understanding the Purpose' broadens the scope of permissible disclosure, making it more difficult to prove a breach of confidentiality. This is particularly problematic in Pennsylvania, where trade secret protection relies on a clear duty to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires 'misappropriation' for a trade secret violation. Misappropriation includes disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that the disclosure was wrongful under the circumstances. The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the level of effort required to prevent disclosure and the necessity of access, making it more difficult to prove the disclosure was wrongful and thus a violation of PUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with Pennsylvania trade secret law. The original text uses 'not disclose' and 'best efforts,' establishing a strong duty to maintain secrecy. The changed text uses 'endeavor not to disclose' and 'reasonable efforts,' lowering the standard of care and introducing ambiguity. Further, the original text limits disclosure to employees who 'need to have access' to 'assist such party in achieving the Purpose', while the changed text broadens the scope to those who 'may have access' to 'assist such party in understanding the Purpose.' According to the Law Explanation, PUTSA requires 'misappropriation,' including wrongful disclosure. By weakening the obligation to protect confidentiality and broadening permissible disclosure, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove wrongful disclosure, potentially undermining PUTSA. 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) discusses damages for misappropriation, implying that a violation of trade secret law (misappropriation) would give rise to damages. The modifications would make it more difficult to prove such a violation.

perturbation.original_text: As a condition to such disclosure, the Receiving Party must inform its employees and representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and the terms of this Agreement and each representative of a Receiving Party that receives Confidential Information must agree in writing to be bound by the terms of this Agreement as is the Receiving Party. Each party hereto is responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its employees and representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: As a condition to such disclosure, the Receiving Party should inform its employees and representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and the terms of this Agreement and each representative of a Receiving Party that receives Confidential Information should acknowledge awareness of the terms of this Agreement. Each party hereto is reasonably responsible for any willful breach of this Agreement by its employees and representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must inform' to 'should inform' and 'must agree in writing to be bound' to 'should acknowledge awareness' weakens the requirement for employee confidentiality agreements. Replacing 'responsible' with 'reasonably responsible' and adding 'willful' limits the liability for breaches. This creates ambiguity and makes it harder to enforce the confidentiality provisions against employees under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on Agency and Contractual Liability
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Pennsylvania agency law, a principal is generally liable for the actions of their agent acting within the scope of their authority. By limiting responsibility to 'willful' breaches and weakening the requirement for binding agreements, the modified text creates uncertainty about the extent to which the Receiving Party is liable for breaches by its employees, which contradicts the general principles of agency law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract by changing 'must inform' to 'should inform' and 'must agree in writing to be bound' to 'should acknowledge awareness.' It also limits liability by changing 'responsible' to 'reasonably responsible' and adding 'willful.' According to the law explanation, this alteration introduces uncertainty regarding the Receiving Party's liability for employee breaches and contradicts agency law. The scraped snippet states that 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal' and 'A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities.' The changed contract weakens the principal's control and responsibility, potentially conflicting with the broad liability established in agency law, especially regarding the actions of employees acting as agents.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination by either party of discussions concerning the Purpose of this Agreement, or sooner if so requested by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all Confidential Information, including all copies of the same. Upon request, the fact of any such destruction must be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an officer of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination by either party of discussions concerning the Purpose of this Agreement, or sooner if reasonably requested by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will endeavor to return to the Disclosing Party or appropriately dispose of all Confidential Information, including all copies of the same. Upon request, confirmation of any such disposal may be provided to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'so requested' to 'reasonably requested' introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'return to the Disclosing Party or destroy' with 'endeavor to return to the Disclosing Party or appropriately dispose of' makes the obligation less definite. Changing 'must be certified in writing' to 'may be provided' makes verification optional. This weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, making it more difficult to enforce, especially concerning trade secrets under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret. The modification makes it harder to prove a 'threatened misappropriation' because the obligation to return or destroy the information is weakened. The changes introduce too much ambiguity, making it harder to demonstrate that the Receiving Party is likely to disclose or use the trade secret improperly because it's only obliged to 'endeavor' to return or 'appropriately dispose of it'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation to protect confidential information, making it harder to enforce trade secret protections under Pennsylvania law. The original text required the Receiving Party to 'return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all Confidential Information' and 'certify in writing' such destruction. The changed text replaces this with an obligation to 'endeavor to return to the Disclosing Party or appropriately dispose of' the information, and 'confirmation of any such disposal may be provided.' This weakens the obligation significantly. The law snippet defines 'misappropriation' to include the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means, or the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By making the return or disposal of confidential information less certain and the verification optional, the modified contract increases the risk of improper use or disclosure, thus undermining the protections intended by trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: In any case, such Receiving Party will (a) disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information which the Receiving Party’s legal counsel advises is required to be disclosed, (b) use be st efforts to ensure that such Confidential Information is treated confidentially, and (c) notify such Disclosing Party as soon as reasonably practicable of the items of Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In any case, such Receiving Party will (a) disclose that portion of the Confidential Information which the Receiving Party’s legal counsel suggests is important to be disclosed, (b) use reasonable efforts to ensure that such Confidential Information is treated with some level of confidentiality, and (c) notify such Disclosing Party when convenient of the items of Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes an obligation to disclose only what counsel 'advises is required'. The changed text allows for disclosure of anything counsel 'suggests is important,' broadening the scope of permissible disclosure. 'Best efforts' is reduced to 'reasonable efforts', and 'treated confidentially' becomes 'treated with some level of confidentiality', and 'as soon as reasonably practicable' turns to 'when convenient'. All these modifications decrease the protection afforded to Confidential Information and increases the ambiguity around the level of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Professional Conduct regarding Attorney-Client Privilege
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6 addresses confidentiality of information related to the representation of a client. While it allows for disclosure to comply with a legal obligation, it does not allow for disclosure simply because counsel suggests it's 'important'. This ambiguous allowance undermines the core principle of attorney-client privilege and client confidentiality.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by introducing ambiguities that undermine client confidentiality, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. The original text requires disclosure only of what counsel 'advises is required,' but the changed text allows disclosure of anything counsel 'suggests is important,' significantly broadening the scope of permissible disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 2 notes that legal ethics broadly refers to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system, including client confidentiality, and that lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. The change from 'best efforts' to 'reasonable efforts,' 'treated confidentially' to 'treated with some level of confidentiality,' and 'as soon as reasonably practicable' to 'when convenient' further weakens the protection of Confidential Information. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, potentially resulting in non-compliance. Therefore, the modified contract introduces ambiguity that undermines the core principle of client confidentiality, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation commenced by a party to enforce this Agreement, the primarily prevailing party will be entitled to recover its costs and expenses in such litigation, including attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation related to this Agreement, the prevailing party may be awarded some of its reasonable costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text guarantees the recovery of costs and attorney fees to the 'primarily prevailing party.' The changed text makes the award of costs discretionary ('may be awarded') and limits it to 'some of its reasonable costs,' eliminating the guarantee of full recovery and creating ambiguity about the extent of recovery. It also removes the 'primarily' aspect of the prevailing party, adding more ambiguity on who would qualify.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Contract Law Regarding Attorney's Fees
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania generally follows the 'American Rule' where each party pays its own attorney's fees, statutes and contracts can alter this rule. If a contract provides for attorney's fees to the prevailing party, courts generally enforce it. The modified clause provides no guarantee of attorneys fees, even to the prevailing party, weakening the contractual right to fees and injecting ambiguity into the enforcement mechanism. It contradicts the general principles on how contracts can provide for attorney's fees. Specifically, the changes would have to be read in conjunction with 42 Pa.C.S. § 1726(a) to determine if attorney fees are awarded in a scenario when the contract only states 'costs'
perturbation.justification: The original contract language guarantees cost and attorney fee recovery to the 'primarily prevailing party.' The modified language makes awarding costs discretionary and limits it to 'some of its reasonable costs,' eliminating the guarantee of full recovery. The Law Explanation discusses how contracts can alter the 'American Rule' by providing for attorney's fees to the prevailing party. The changed text weakens the contractual right to fees and injects ambiguity into the enforcement mechanism. This constitutes a contradiction because the modified clause would have to be read in conjunction with 42 Pa.C.S. § 1726(a) to determine if attorney fees are awarded in a scenario when the contract only states 'costs.'
